chunk_summary: This section outlines the triage process for severe acute malnutrition (SAM), emphasizing the distinction between complicated cases requiring inpatient care and uncomplicated cases eligible for outpatient management.
learning_objectives: Understand the criteria for classifying SAM as complicated or uncomplicated.
Learn the clinical signs and danger indicators for complicated SAM requiring inpatient care.
Recognize the importance of reassessing SAM classification at every contact.
Understand the risks of fluid resuscitation in severely malnourished children.
key_facts: Complicated SAM includes IMNCI danger signs, severe illness (e.g., pneumonia), dehydration, persistent vomiting, altered sensorium, hypothermia, severe anemia, or failed appetite test.
Uncomplicated SAM requires alertness, clinical stability, no danger signs, and passing a standardized appetite test.
Sepsis and pneumonia in SAM may present with minimal fever; low admission thresholds are recommended.
Fluid resuscitation in SAM must be cautious to avoid heart failure; prioritize antibiotics, warming, and glucose over rapid boluses.
Reassess SAM classification at every contact due to the risk of rapid deterioration.
Evidence-based supportive care measures are essential
reference_quote: Classify as complicated SAM if any of the following are present: IMNCI general danger signs, severe illness classification (e.g., severe pneumonia), dehydration with diarrhea, persistent vomiting, altered sensorium/apathy, hypothermia, severe pallor/anemia, extensive skin or eye lesions, post-measles state, severe bilateral oedema, or failed appetite test. (Source: IMNCI Chart Booklet for Medical Officers, 2023)
document_key_topics: Criteria for complicated versus uncomplicated SAM
Clinical management and triage guidelines for SAM
Risks and precautions in fluid therapy for malnourished children
